title: Simple Build Bed Frame (All Mattress Sizes)
summary: A paneled-headboard bed from framing lumber — no pocket holes, no joinery, just self-tapping screws. Sized here for queen, with the twin and full numbers in the notes.
description:
This is the bed frame for someone who has never built a bed frame. Every joint is a self-tapping screw driven through one board into another — no pocket holes, no dowels, nothing hidden. The design gets its strength from where the screws go, not from clever joinery: the legs sandwich the side rails, the 2x2 cleats carry the slats, and a 2x4 cap on the headboard keeps the paneling from warping.

Two decisions matter before you buy wood. First, size: the cut list below is for a queen (80" x 60") — twin and full change only the lengths, and the notes carry those numbers. Full, queen, and king also need a center support rail and leg; a twin doesn't span far enough to need one. Second, the headboard paneling: 1x6 boards give the farmhouse plank look, but any 3/4" material works — plywood for a smooth painted panel, shiplap if you want the shadow lines.

Stain or paint the boards before assembly. It sounds fussy, but a headboard is a grid of inside corners after it's together, and every one of them is miserable to brush. Finish flat boards, assemble, then touch up the cut ends.

tools.note: Every cut is a straight crosscut. A miter saw is faster if you have one.
Self-tapping star-bit screws do all the joinery — a driver with a clutch saves you from burying heads.
Framing lumber arrives rough. Sand before finishing or the stain will show every mill mark.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs, headboard top, and center support. Pick yellow-toned 'whitewood' — it's easier to drive screws into than fir. Twin needs only 2.

materials.name: 2x6, 12 ft
materials.note: Side rails plus headboard/footboard rails. Twin can use 10-footers.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Slat cleats and headboard back cleats.

materials.name: 1x6, 10 ft
materials.note: Headboard paneling — each 10-footer yields two 57" panels. Any 3/4" material works: plywood for smooth, shiplap for farmhouse.

materials.name: 1x4, 10 ft
materials.note: Bed slats — two 57" slats per board. 1x3s or strips of 3/4" plywood work too.

materials.name: Self-tapping screws, #8 x 1-3/4", star drive
materials.note: For attaching the 1x6 paneling and 1x4 slats.

materials.name: Stain or paint
materials.note: Apply to the boards before assembly; touch up cut ends after.

cutList.note: 72" for twin/full. These run inside the side rails and carry every slat.
40" twin, 55" full. Not decorative — it keeps the paneling from warping.
Source lists 10, but at 80" of mattress length 12 gives sane spacing — match the queen shopping list. 36" twin, 51" full (10 slats).
Center rail — full/queen/king only. 75" for full.
Full/queen/king only. King needs two center supports.

steps.title: Build the two leg assemblies in mirror
steps.body:
Each side of the bed is a leg set: headboard leg, side rail, footboard leg. Build them as mirror images of each other — lay them side by side and check before driving a single screw, because it's genuinely easy to build two lefts.

Attach the legs to the side rails with the 2-1/2" screws, two or three per joint.

steps.title: Add the footboard fronts and headboard cleats
steps.body: Screw the 7" 2x4 pieces to the fronts of the footboard legs, driving the long screws from the back so nothing shows. Then add the 2x2 back cleats to the headboard legs, flush to the outside edges — these are what the paneling lands on later.

steps.title: Attach the slat cleats
steps.body:
Run the long 2x2 cleats along the inside faces of the side rails with the 2-1/2" screws. Their height sets your mattress height — flush the cleat bottom with the rail bottom and the slats sit low, which most mattresses want.

Screw generously. Every night, the entire load of the mattress and its occupants goes through these two sticks.

steps.title: Assemble the bed in the room
steps.body:
Carry the pieces into the bedroom before you tie them together — a finished queen frame does not go around a stairwell corner. Attach the headboard and footboard rails to the side rail assemblies with the long screws.

Check the frame is square by measuring both diagonals; shove the long corner until they match.

steps.title: Cap the headboard and add the paneling
steps.body:
Screw the 2x4 cap flat across the top of the headboard. This isn't trim — five 1x6s screwed only at their ends will cup and twist with the seasons, and the cap ties them all to something rigid.

Then screw the paneling boards on from the back with the 1-3/4" screws, so the headboard face shows no fasteners at all.

steps.title: Add the center support (full size and up)
steps.body:
A twin mattress spans 39" and the slats handle that alone. Anything wider needs help: build the center rail with its leg, and add the support cleat at the headboard end. King beds need two of these rails.

Skip this and the bed will work fine — for a month. Then the slats start to bow and squeak.

steps.title: Lay and screw the slats
steps.body: Cut the slats, space them evenly along the cleats — a gap of 2–3" between slats is right for foam mattresses — and screw each end down with the shorter screws. One screw per end is enough; the screw's job is to stop the slat wandering, not to carry load.

images.alt: Wood bed frame with plank-paneled headboard built from construction lumber
